model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model for chronic shoulder/jaw tension with low interoceptive awareness and tension-triggered headaches. Preserves 6 criterion nodes (C01–C06) and 8 modifiable predictors (P01–P08). Design: 28-day EMA with 6 momentary prompts/day + 1 morning diary; passive jaw-clench sensor aggregated per prompt. Variables chosen to maximize within-person variability and coverage of BIO-PSYCHO-SOCIAL levers (PMR, interoception/mindfulness, breathing, sleep, posture/ergonomics, passive bruxism signal, microbreaks, cognitive reappraisal). Dense plausibility matrices provided for P->C, P->P and C->C. Sparse directed edges select the top incoming relations per target (2–4 for P->C; 1–3 for P->P and C->C) and copy the dense values exactly.
variable_selection_notes: Kept all 6 clinically-relevant criteria from the case. Selected 8 predictors prioritized by the hyde signals (mindfulness/interoception, PMR, breathing, wearable jaw sensor, sleep, posture cues, micro-breaks, cognitive reappraisal). All momentary self-report items use 1–9 anchored Likert where feasible (to increase variance). Sleep is measured as a morning diary (last-night). Passive jaw-clench count is aggregated per EMA prompt. Total nodes = 14 (<= 18).

momentary_ema.notes: 6 daily prompts provide dense within-day resolution consistent with expected short lags (0–6h). Use push notifications with 15-minute response window.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures last-night sleep quality and other 24h summary items.

passive_streams.notes: Jaw clench/bruxism sensor events aggregated into count per EMA window; wearable HRV optional but not included to limit nodes.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Design provides sufficient repeated measures per node (>=168 prompts, 28 diaries). Momentary constructs likely to show within-person variance; passive jaw-count will provide additional objective variance. Expect to detrend daily cycles (time-of-day) during preprocessing.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Most momentary self-report items expected high within-person variability (tension, threat expectation, awareness). Sleep (morning) provides between-day variance. Passive jaw sensor expected high-resolution variability.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: 6 prompts/day is moderate burden; expect 60–80% compliance. Prioritize PMR and sleep adherence reminders to reduce biased missingness; include passive sensor to mitigate self-report gaps.

safety_notes: No diagnostic claims. If headaches are severe, frequent, or accompanied by neurological signs (visual change, weakness, confusion), advise seeking urgent medical evaluation. If anticipatory threat or anxiety is disabling, recommend contacting a mental health professional. Passive sensor deployment must follow privacy/consent procedures.

edges.notes: PMR practice expected to rapidly reduce momentary muscle tension.
Objective jaw-clench events strongly predict tension-type headache intensity in short lags.
Interoceptive practice should increase momentary awareness (reduce low-awareness score).
Cognitive reappraisal is expected to reduce anticipatory threat expectation in short lags.
Breathing micro-practices reduce difficulty relaxing, especially acutely.
Ergonomic/posture adjustments expected to reduce habitual bracing over short periods.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (PMR), P03 (breathing) and P02 (interoception) may correlate closely (downregulation cluster).
P05 (posture) and P07 (micro-breaks) may be correlated because breaks often entail posture change.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C04 (anticipatory threat) and C01 (tension) may show elevated baseline (ceiling) in some participants.
P06 (jaw sensor) may have many zeros for some users (floor) if device not worn or no events.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use momentary 1-9 scales to increase variance for subjective states.
Encourage wearing of passive sensor to avoid floor effects; consider log-transform for counts.
Include time-of-day covariates and day-of-week to model diurnal cycles (detrending).
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Caffeine/hydration nodes (excluded to limit node count).
Detailed medication/pharmacotherapy variables (excluded due to scope and safety).
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 14 nodes and 168 momentary prompts + 28 diaries, gVAR estimation should be feasible for pairwise VAR with regularization. Preprocessing should include per-person centering, detrending (time-of-day), and handling zero-inflation for passive counts.

source_variable.label: Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw

measurement.item_or_signal: Self-rated shoulder and jaw tension right now
measurement.notes: Primary momentary symptom; anchors emphasize shoulders and jaw.

criteria.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension
criteria.query_text_used: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension | Operational item/signal: Since last prompt, how much have you noticed bodily tension or tightness before it led to another symptom? | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Scale reversed purposely (higher=lower awareness) to match criterion framing.

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: 'reduced_level_of_awareness' corresponds to the variable's focus on low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension. The measurement item asks about noticing bodily tension since the last prompt, and the reversal of the 1–9 scale (higher = lower awareness) aligns with the idea of reduced awareness. The leaf explicitly encodes a diminished level of awareness, which is the closest explicit counterpart among candidates for 'Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension' given the EMA_self_report, momentary design and the bodily focus of the variable. While the leaf appears under delirium/neurocognitive domains, it best captures the intended operational construct (low awareness) among available options; no leaf perfectly encodes 'low awareness of bodily tension' specifically.

mapping.leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / neurocognitive disorders / delirium / due to another medical condition / reduced_level_of_awareness

source_variable.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension

measurement.item_or_signal: Since last prompt, how much have you noticed bodily tension or tightness before it led to another symptom?
measurement.notes: Scale reversed purposely (higher=lower awareness) to match criterion framing.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: invert or recode if needed; center per-person

criteria.label: Headaches triggered by muscular tension
criteria.query_text_used: Headaches triggered by muscular tension | Operational item/signal: Since last prompt, have you experienced a headache and its intensity? | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Headache intensity used as the continuous criterion; flag can be recorded separately but not a node.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Headaches triggered by muscular tension' maps best to tension-type headache as a clinical phenotype. The chosen leaf 16165 represents tension-type headache with mild_to_moderate_intensity, aligning with the measurement of headache intensity (1–9 Likert) used in EMA. Pericranial muscle tenderness (another candidate) reflects a mechanism/signal rather than the headache diagnosis itself, making tension-type headache the more precise fit for a criterion leaf.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since last prompt, have you experienced a headache and its intensity?
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: Two parts: (A) Headache flag (yes/no) optional; (B) Intensity 1-9 Likert (1=none, 9=very severe). Use intensity as node.
measurement.notes: Headache intensity used as the continuous criterion; flag can be recorded separately but not a node.

criteria.label: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation
criteria.query_text_used: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation | Operational item/signal: Right now, how much do you expect that something bad is about to happen? | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Key cognitive-affective criterion tied to bracing.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Persistent anticipatory threat expectation' maps to a cognitive-affective anticipatory fear construct. The EMA item 'Right now, how much do you expect that something bad is about to happen?' is a momentary assessment of imminent threat expectancy. Among candidates, the closest leaf is 'fear_of_something_bad_may_happen' (depressive disorder with anxious distress specifier), which directly encodes anticipatory threat expectation; selected as the most specific operational leaf given the available taxonomy. This aligns with the bracing mechanism in the pseudoprofile context as a cognitive appraisals component that increases tension.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how much do you expect that something bad is about to happen?
measurement.notes: Key cognitive-affective criterion tied to bracing.

criteria.label: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days | Operational item/signal: Since last prompt, how difficult has it been to relax given the current context? | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Collect perceived objective contextual stress as a separate optional item (not a node) if needed.

mapping.rationale: This variable is a momentary self-report of how difficult it is to relax in the current context (EMA, 1–9 scale). The closest CRITERION leaf is 5618 'emotional relaxation' which directly targets the relaxation state within the emotional domain. It matches the measurement mode (EMA_self_report via mobile_app) and the item intent (perceived relaxation state rather than a technique or life-stress focus). Other leaves emphasize relaxation techniques or broader stress-management constructs, making 5618 the most specific operational counterpart.

criteria.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat
criteria.query_text_used: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat | Operational item/signal: Since last prompt, to what extent did you find yourself automatically bracing/tensing (posture/face)? | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Complemented by passive jaw sensor count for objective bracing signal.

mapping.rationale: Variable label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing involves automatic muscle tension in posture/face. The EMA self-report item asks how much one automatically braced/tensed since last prompt, which targets momentary muscle tension and posture control. The measurement uses mobile app prompts (6 per day) and is complemented by a passive jaw sensor; thus the best matching leaf should capture posture-focused bracing measurement with potential integration of posture-monitoring instruments. The leaf 'posture monitoring devices' (idx 5124) corresponds to posture awareness and devices that monitor posture, aligning with both the measurement modality (self-report + sensor) and the mechanism (posture-related bracing). Other leaves refer to sleep bruxism, clinical movement disorders, or cognitive constructs (anticipatory vigilance), which are less specific to daytime habitual bracing measured via EMA.

source_variable.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat

measurement.item_or_signal: Since last prompt, to what extent did you find yourself automatically bracing/tensing (posture/face)?
measurement.notes: Complemented by passive jaw sensor count for objective bracing signal.

predictors.label: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice intensity since last prompt
predictors.query_text_used: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice intensity since last prompt | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: PMR directly reduces peripheral muscle tension and may lower headache likelihood and general arousal. | Intervention examples: Guided PMR focusing on shoulders, neck, jaw; 10–20 min sessions. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How much PMR practice (short guided or self) did you do since last prompt? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf: 'progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice' under PSYCHO / breath_and_relaxation_skills. The variable PMR practice intensity since last prompt is an EMA self-report about PMR engagement, aligning with measurement.item_or_signal 'progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice' and measurement.recall_window 'since_last_prompt'. Mechanism rationale matches: PMR directly reduces peripheral muscle tension, potentially lowering arousal and headache likelihood. The ontology_path mirrors the intended construct (PMR as a physiological relaxation skill within psychological skillkits).

source_variable.label: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice intensity since last prompt
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: PMR directly reduces peripheral muscle tension and may lower headache likelihood and general arousal.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Guided PMR focusing on shoulders, neck, jaw; 10–20 min sessions.

measurement.item_or_signal: How much PMR practice (short guided or self) did you do since last prompt?
measurement.notes: Encourages reporting minutes via categorical anchors; expected to fluctuate with routine.

expected_effects.rationale: Immediate downregulation of peripheral muscle tension after PMR.
Regular PMR reduces headache-triggering muscle tension over hours to days.

feasibility.notes: Self-report feasible; may need reminders for adherence.

This mapping supports P02 as a modifiable predictor addressing interoceptive awareness (C02).

predictors.query_text_used: Diaphragmatic/soothing breathing micro-practices since last prompt | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Quick autonomic downshift that transiently lowers muscle tension and threat-related arousal. | Intervention examples: 30s–2min paced breathing exercises. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since last prompt, how much breathing practice did you do? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / breath_and_relaxation_skills / *diaphragmatic_breathing_practice

mapping.rationale: Variable P03 indicates diaphragmatic/soothing breathing micro-practices. The candidate leaf 3055 exactly matches diaphragmatic_breathing_practice under breath_and_relaxation_skills, within the PSYCHO domain. Measurement is EMA_self_report with 'since_last_prompt,' aligning with 'since last prompt' assessment. Mechanism matches quick autonomic downshift reducing muscle tension/arousal. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates for diaphragmatic breathing; other options (e.g., paced_breathing, soothing_rhythm_breathing) are broader or less specific to diaphragmatic technique.

The mechanism rationale fits: mindfulness-based posture checks can increase awareness of musculoskeletal load and prompt corrective adjustments, reducing shoulder/jaw tension. This aligns with the pseudoprofile emphasis on interoception/mindfulness and posture cues as BIOxPSYCHO levers.

source_variable.label: Ergonomic/posture adjustments and mindful posture checks since last prompt
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Posture adjustments reduce mechanical load and local muscle tension in shoulders/jaw.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Seat setup, posture cue prompts, neck/shoulder stretches.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since last prompt, how much did you do posture/ergonomic adjustments or posture checks?
measurement.notes: Captures both behavior and environmental adjustments.

feasibility.notes: Self-report feasible; objective ergonomic metrics not included to limit nodes.

mapping.rationale: Variable label indicates passive jaw-clench/bruxism sensor event count per EMA window. The candidate leaf 4564 corresponds exactly to BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / neurophysiology_and_stress_proxies / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor, whose measurement item is Jaw clench/bruxism_sensor. source_variable.label: Passive jaw-clench/bruxism sensor event count (per EMA window)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Objective proxy for jaw/face muscle activation; indicates covert bracing and can trigger interventions.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Use as passive feed; optionally enable biofeedback prompts.

measurement.item_or_signal: Jaw clench/bruxism event count in last EMA window
measurement.notes: Objective measure; may complement self-report bracing (C06).

expected_effects.rationale: Higher bruxism counts are strongly associated with tension-type headaches in short lags.

feasibility.notes: Requires user device/sensor; provides objective variance to support gVAR estimation.

predictors.label: Hourly micro-breaks with movement since last prompt
predictors.query_text_used: Hourly micro-breaks with movement since last prompt | Domain: PSYCHOxSOCIAL | Mechanism: Frequent micro-breaks reduce posture-related load and transient tension accumulation. | Intervention examples: 1–2 minute stretch/movement breaks. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since last prompt, how many micro-breaks with movement did you take? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / flexible_skill_selection_in_context / *early_warning_signs_to_action_routine

mapping.rationale: The variable P07 measures 'Hourly micro-breaks with movement since last prompt' captured via EMA as 'Since last prompt, how many micro-breaks with movement did you take?' with sampling 6 times per day. The best matching leaf is micro_restoration_routines (PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / recovery_design / restoration_and_self_care / *micro_restoration_routines) because it directly denotes short, restorative micro-breaks intended to interrupt ongoing activity and reduce tension, aligning with the mechanism that frequent micro-breaks mitigate posture-related load and transient tension accumulation. Other candidates emphasize mindfulness, posture cues, or general practices that are less specifically about counting micro-breaks, making micro_restoration_routines the most specific operational match.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / recovery_design / restoration_and_self_care / micro_restoration_routines

source_variable.label: Threat reappraisal / cognitive reframing practice since last prompt
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Cognitive reappraisal reduces anticipatory threat expectation and consequent bracing.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief reappraisal prompts or reframing exercises.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since last prompt, how much did you use cognitive reappraisal/ threat reappraisal strategies?
measurement.notes: Targets cognitive processes sustaining bracing and threat expectation.

feasibility.notes: Self-report of skill use; training may be needed prior to measurement.
